text.title: VUI Principle Rules and Known Failure Modes
text.summary: Testable rules derived from the three VUI principles, with LLM-specific failure modes and latency constraints.

rule.statement: No agent turn may exceed 25 words unless the content is a required legal disclosure; flag any turn over 25 words for simplification review.
rule.rationale: 25 words is approximately the working memory capacity for spoken information; longer turns cause information loss and user re-prompting.

rule.statement: Do not include more than one proactive offer per turn (e.g., "Want to know about tomorrow?" is one offer; adding "Shall I also check traffic?" in the same turn is too many choices).
rule.rationale: Multiple simultaneous offers force the user to hold all options in working memory; one clear offer per turn keeps the conversation linear.

rule.statement: Write acceptance criteria for voice flows that include a latency gate: total round-trip must be under 1.5 seconds; streams must produce first audio within 0.8 seconds.
rule.rationale: Users interpret gaps beyond 1.5 s as system failure; without a latency gate in acceptance criteria the constraint is never tested.

antipattern.description: LLM over-acknowledges every user input with affirmations ("Great!", "Of course!", "Sure thing!").
antipattern.reason: Each filler word adds TTS latency and signals non-human origin; instruct the model explicitly to omit acknowledgment before answering.

antipattern.description: Simplicity principle applied to chat or visual copy, producing text that is too terse for reading contexts.
antipattern.reason: VUI simplicity rules are calibrated for audio processing, not reading; a 10-word sentence that is ideal for voice can be insufficient for a UI label.

antipattern.description: Agent applies these principles to a mandatory regulatory disclosure script (banking, healthcare).
antipattern.reason: Regulatory disclosures must be verbatim; "natural conversation" and "simplicity" cannot override legal text requirements.

section.title: Cultural and Linguistic Scope
section.p: These principles are derived from US English voice UX research. Conversational norms, turn-taking conventions, and acceptable response lengths vary significantly across languages and cultures. Apply only after localization review for non-English deployments.
